The **Marylebone Cricket Club** (**MCC**) is a world-famous [cricket club](/wiki/Cricket_club "Cricket club") founded in 1787, whose [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") headquarters are dubbed "the Home of Cricket".

[Lord's Cricket Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") has been owned since Victorian times by MCC, which served as [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket")'s governing body from 1788 to 1909 and retains considerable global influence.[[1]](#cite_note-1) [Thomas Lord](/wiki/Thomas_Lord "Thomas Lord") first established a cricket ground at [Dorset Fields](/wiki/Dorset_Square "Dorset Square") in [Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone "Marylebone"). [Lord's Cricket Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") relocated in 1814 to nearby [St John's Wood](/wiki/St_John%27s_Wood "St John's Wood"), historically in [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex "Middlesex") and now in the [City of Westminster](/wiki/City_of_Westminster "City of Westminster"), [London NW8](/wiki/North_London "North London").

In 1788, the MCC assumed oversight for the *[Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")*, issuing a revised version that year. Changes to these *Laws* are nowadays determined by the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), with copyright retained by MCC.[[2]](#cite_note-Laws-2) Established in 1909, the ICC was administered for eighty years by the Secretary to Marylebone Cricket Club and chaired by the MCC President *ex officio*.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

MCC was given responsibility for organising [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") [overseas tours](/wiki/International_cricket "International cricket") commencing with the [1903–04 tour of Australia](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_Australia_in_1903%E2%80%9304 "English cricket team in Australia in 1903–04") and ending with its [1976–77 tour of India](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_India_and_Sri_Lanka_in_1976%E2%80%9377 "English cricket team in India and Sri Lanka in 1976–77"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) both being victorious. England also played non-international matches overseas under the [auspices](/wiki/Auspices "Auspices") of MCC.

In 1993, MCC transferred global cricket governance to the [International Cricket Council](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council") (ICC), devolving domestic administration to the [Test and County Cricket Board](/wiki/Test_and_County_Cricket_Board "Test and County Cricket Board") (TCCB).

Retaining [first-class status](/wiki/First-class_cricket "First-class cricket") when MCC plays first-class opposition, the club continues to promote the game by fielding *ad hoc* MCC XIs from a pool of circa 2,000 playing members, varying standards accordingly.

The outgoing [MCC President](/wiki/President_(corporate_title) "President (corporate title)"), the [Lord King of Lothbury](/wiki/Mervyn_King,_Baron_King_of_Lothbury "Mervyn King, Baron King of Lothbury") [KG](/wiki/Knight_of_the_Order_of_the_Garter "Knight of the Order of the Garter"),[[6]](#cite_note-6) nominated [Ed Smith](/wiki/Ed_Smith_(cricketer) "Ed Smith (cricketer)") as his successor for 2026.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## History and role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and role")]

The origin of MCC was as a [gentlemen's club](/wiki/Gentlemen%27s_club "Gentlemen's club") that had flourished through most of the 18th century, including, at least in part, an existence as the original [London Cricket Club](/wiki/London_Cricket_Club "London Cricket Club"), which played at the [HAC](/wiki/Honourable_Artillery_Company "Honourable Artillery Company") [cricket ground](/wiki/Artillery_Ground "Artillery Ground") in the mid-1700s. Many of its members left for the [Hambledon Club](/wiki/Hambledon_Club "Hambledon Club") during the 1770s and then, in the early 1780s, returned to London's environs when the [White Conduit Club](/wiki/White_Conduit_Club "White Conduit Club") began playing cricket in [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington"). It is not known for certain when the White Conduit was established but it seems to have been after 1780 and certainly by 1785. According to [Sir Pelham Warner](/wiki/Pelham_Warner "Pelham Warner"), it was formed before 1782 as an offshoot from a [West End](/wiki/West_End_of_London "West End of London") [convivial club](/wiki/Gentlemen%27s_club "Gentlemen's club") called the *Je-ne-sais-Quoi*, some of whose members frequented the [White Conduit House](/wiki/White_Conduit_House "White Conduit House") in [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington") and played matches on the neighbouring [White Conduit Fields](/wiki/White_Conduit_Fields "White Conduit Fields"), a prominent venue for cricket in the 1720s.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Arthur Haygarth](/wiki/Arthur_Haygarth "Arthur Haygarth") states in *Scores and Biographies* that "the Marylebone Club was founded in 1787 from White Conduit members" but the date when it was established "could not be found".[[9]](#cite_note-9) This gentlemen's club convened for multi-purpose social meetings at the *Star & Garter Inn* on [Pall Mall](/wiki/Pall_Mall,_London "Pall Mall, London"). Having undertaken to draft the *[Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")* at various times, most notably in 1744 and 1774, the White Conduit soon vested such law-making with the MCC as the final repose of cricketing gentlemen. At the White Conduit Club's outset, its leading lights were [George Finch, 9th Earl of Winchilsea and Nottingham](/wiki/George_Finch,_9th_Earl_of_Winchilsea "George Finch, 9th Earl of Winchilsea") (1752–1826) and [Colonel the Hon. Charles Lennox](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_4th_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 4th Duke of Richmond") (1764–1819), who later became the [4th Duke of Richmond](/wiki/Duke_of_Richmond "Duke of Richmond"). The White Conduit was nominally an exclusive club that only "[gentlemen](/wiki/Amateur_status_in_first-class_cricket "Amateur status in first-class cricket")" could play for, but the club did engage [professionals](/wiki/Given_man "Given man") and one of these was [Thomas Lord](/wiki/Thomas_Lord "Thomas Lord"), recognised for his business acumen (being a successful [wine](/wiki/Vintner "Vintner") [merchant](/wiki/Mercer_(occupation) "Mercer (occupation)")) "as well as his bowling ability".[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-DB47-11)

The New Club may have continued there, save that White Conduit Fields was an [open area](/wiki/Common_land "Common land") where the public, including rowdier types, could watch cricket matches and voice their opinions on the play and players. The White Conduit members were not amused by such interruptions and decided to seek their own enclosed venue.[[11]](#cite_note-DB47-11) Lord Winchilsea and Colonel Lennox asked Lord to find a new ground offering him a guarantee against any losses he may suffer in the venture.[[12]](#cite_note-HSA51-12)[[11]](#cite_note-DB47-11) Lord took a lease from the [Portman Estate](/wiki/Portman_Estate "Portman Estate") on Dorset Fields where [Dorset Square](/wiki/Dorset_Square "Dorset Square") is now sited; and prepared [the ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Old_Ground "Lord's Old Ground") opening in 1787. Initially called the "New Cricket Ground", being nearby "the New Road" in [Marylebone](/wiki/Marylebone "Marylebone") when the first-known match was played there on 21 May, by the end of July 1787, it was known as *Lord's*.[[13]](#cite_note-GB110-13) As it was in Marylebone, the White Conduit members who relocated there soon decided to rename themselves the "Mary-le-bone Club".[[14]](#cite_note-GB115-14) The exact date of MCC's foundation is lost but seems to have been sometime in the late spring or the summer of 1787.[[15]](#cite_note-PW18-15) On 10 & 11 July 1837, a [South v North](/wiki/North_v_South "North v South") match was staged at Lord's to commemorate the MCC Golden Jubilee. Warner described it as "a Grand Match to celebrate the Jubilee of the Club" and reproduced the full scorecard.[[15]](#cite_note-PW18-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

On 25 April 1787, London's *[Morning Herald](/wiki/Morning_Herald "Morning Herald")* newspaper carried a notice: "The Members of the Cricket Club are desired to meet at the *Star & Garter*, Pall Mall, on Mon., April 30. Dinner on table exactly at half past five o'clock. N.B. The favour of an answer is desired".[[13]](#cite_note-GB110-13) Its agenda unspecified, only three weeks later on Saturday, 19 May, the *Morning Herald* advertised: "A grand match will be played on Monday, 21 May in the New Cricket Ground, the New Road, Mary-le-bone, between eleven Noblemen of the White Conduit Club and eleven Gentlemen of the County of Middlesex with two men given, for 500 guineas a side. The wickets to be pitched at ten o'clock, and the match to be played out". No post-match report has yet been found but, as [G. B. Buckley](/wiki/G._B._Buckley "G. B. Buckley") states, it was "apparently the first match to be played on Lord's new ground".[[13]](#cite_note-GB110-13)

A total of eight matches are known to have been played at Lord's in 1787, one of them a [single-wicket](/wiki/Single_wicket_cricket "Single wicket cricket") event. The only one featuring the Mary-le-bone Club took place on Monday, 30 July. It was advertised in *The World* on Friday, 27 July 1787: "On Monday, 30 July will be played (at Lord's) a match between 11 gentlemen of the Mary-le-bone Club and 11 gentlemen of the Islington Club".[[14]](#cite_note-GB115-14) "This is the earliest notice of the Marylebone Club" according to Buckley[[14]](#cite_note-GB115-14) and as with Lord's inaugural fixture, no post-match report of MCC's inaugural game survives.

## Grounds

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Grounds")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Dorset_square_plaque.jpg/250px-Dorset_square_plaque.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dorset_square_plaque.jpg)

A plaque in Dorset Square, marking the site of the original Lord's Ground, commemorates MCC's foundation in 1787

There have been three Lord's cricket grounds: the original on the [Portman Estate](/wiki/Viscount_Portman "Viscount Portman") and two on the [Eyre Estate](/wiki/Oliver_Crosthwaite-Eyre "Oliver Crosthwaite-Eyre"). All three sites lie to the west of [Regent's Park](/wiki/Regent%27s_Park "Regent's Park") in London. Thomas Lord leased the original ground, now referred to as [Lord's Old Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Old_Ground "Lord's Old Ground"), from the Portman Estate in 1787 and MCC played there until 1810 when Lord, after objecting to a rent increase, decided on terminating the lease to lift his turf and move out.[[15]](#cite_note-PW18-15) Over 200 matches were known to be played there, mostly involving MCC and/or [Middlesex](/wiki/Middlesex_county_cricket_team_(pre-1864) "Middlesex county cricket team (pre-1864)") XIs. Lord's Old Ground was on the site now occupied by [Dorset Square](/wiki/Dorset_Square "Dorset Square"), just east of [Marylebone Station](/wiki/Marylebone_Station "Marylebone Station") and west of [Baker Street](/wiki/Baker_Street "Baker Street"). To commemorate MCC's association, a plaque was unveiled at Dorset Square on 9 May 2006 by [Sir Andrew Strauss](/wiki/Sir_Andrew_Strauss "Sir Andrew Strauss").

With advance knowledge of the Portman Estate's intention to lease its land for building houses commanding much higher rents of over £600 per annum,[[15]](#cite_note-PW18-15) on 15 October 1808, Lord secured two fields on the North Bank area of the St John's Wood Estate, which belonged to Walpole Eyre (1773–1856), a local landowner after whom [Eyre's Tunnel](/wiki/Eyre%27s_Tunnel "Eyre's Tunnel") on the [Regent's Canal](/wiki/Regent%27s_Canal "Regent's Canal") was named.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Lord negotiated with the Eyres a rent of only £54 per annum for a term of eighty years, free of both land taxes and tithes.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The new ground on the Eyre Estate was available from 1809 and so Lord had two cricket fields at his disposal for the 1809 and 1810 seasons. This North Bank ground was sub-let to [St John's Wood Cricket Club](/wiki/St_John%27s_Wood_Cricket_Club "St John's Wood Cricket Club") which eventually merged with MCC.[[19]](#cite_note-PW19-19) Lord formally took over management of this second ground on 8 May 1811 there relaying his turf from the Old Ground. He did this so that "the noblemen and gentlemen of the MCC should be able to play on the same footing as before".[[19]](#cite_note-PW19-19) According to Warner, Lord's relocation proved unpopular among many MCC members with the upshot that the club played no matches there throughout 1811 and 1812.[[19]](#cite_note-PW19-19) Whilst its situation may have not been ideal, cricket generally was in decline at the time because of the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"); the [Association of Cricket Statisticians and Historians](/wiki/Association_of_Cricket_Statisticians_and_Historians "Association of Cricket Statisticians and Historians") (the ACS) asserts that "(from) 1810 to 1814 the game was all but dead", largely because of the War and "the very real threat of civil unrest in England".[[20]](#cite_note-ACS4-20) This second venue is generally known as [Lord's Middle Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Middle_Ground "Lord's Middle Ground"). In the three years that Lord leased it, only six matches were known to have taken place, with just three (all in 1813) involving the MCC. Although the exact location of Lord's Middle Ground remains uncertain, it is understood to have been at North Bank to the north end of [Lisson Grove](/wiki/Lisson_Grove "Lisson Grove") where the [Regent's Canal](/wiki/Regent%27s_Canal "Regent's Canal") cut through. Thus situated partially on the canal's route somewhere in the area now bounded by Lisson Grove (the B507) to south-west, Lodge Road to north-west, Park Road ([the A41](/wiki/A41_road "A41 road")) to north-east and the Regent's Canal to south-east, this cricket field was no further than 300 [yards](/wiki/Yard "Yard") (270 [m](/wiki/Metre "Metre")) from the present [Lord's Cricket Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground "Lord's Cricket Ground").[[19]](#cite_note-PW19-19)

Lord was forced to abandon plans for his Middle Ground after [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") decreed the new canal's route in 1813. Lord, via his protégé [Lord Frederick Beauclerk](/wiki/Lord_Frederick_Beauclerk "Lord Frederick Beauclerk"), approached the Eyre family who agreed to lease another plot nearby in [St John's Wood](/wiki/St_John%27s_Wood "St John's Wood"), but at an increased rent of £100 per annum. Accepting the Eyres' new terms, Lord again relocated his turf which was re-laid in time for the start of the 1814 season. This third ground remains [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") present home to MCC for over 200 years.[[19]](#cite_note-PW19-19)

## *Laws of Cricket*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Laws of Cricket")]

Main article: [Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")

MCC maintains responsibility for, and remains copyright holder of, the *[Laws of Cricket](/wiki/Laws_of_Cricket "Laws of Cricket")*. Its Laws Sub-Committee is responsible for debating and drafting changes to the Laws, with the Main Committee then voting on any proposed amendments.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Membership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/MCC_member.jpg/250px-MCC_member.jpg)](/wiki/File:MCC_member.jpg)

MCC member sporting the club's distinctive [blazer](/wiki/Blazer "Blazer")

MCC has 18,000 full members and 5,000 associate members. Full Members enjoy voting rights and have access to the [Pavilion](/wiki/Lord%27s_Pavilion "Lord's Pavilion") and other stands at [Lord's Cricket Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground "Lord's Cricket Ground") for all matches played there; Associate Members' privileges are more restricted and vary on a case-by-case basis, but in broad terms they can attend most matches except major internationals.[[24]](#cite_note-HTJ-24)

MCC members did not allow female membership until 1998, with previous club ballots on change failing to achieve the requisite two-thirds majority for implementation.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The move to change was spearheaded by [Rachael Heyhoe Flint](/wiki/Rachael_Heyhoe_Flint "Rachael Heyhoe Flint") who applied as "R Flint" to slip under the radar into the male-only application system.[[26]](#cite_note-26) When [Colin Ingleby-Mackenzie](/wiki/Colin_Ingleby-Mackenzie "Colin Ingleby-Mackenzie"), a longstanding advocate for women's membership, assumed the MCC Presidency in 1996 he led a two-year campaign to convince the membership to vote in favour of change. In September 1998 a 70% majority of members voted to allow female membership, so ending 212 years of male exclusivity, and 10 honorary life members were immediately admitted, including Baroness Heyhoe Flint. Until this time, [the Queen](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II"), as club patron, was the only woman (other than domestic staff) permitted to enter the Pavilion during play.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In February 1999, five women were elected as playing members.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

To join the candidates' waiting list for MCC membership one must be proposed by a full member (who can make one nomination per year) who details the prospective candidate's cricketing ability/experience/knowledge *etc*, corroborated with the nominee's own statement accordingly. If MCC deems the nomination form satisfactory, prospective candidates are invited to attend an interview at Lord's with two MCC Committee Members (comprising all MCC Sub-Committees; MCC Committee; MCC Out-Match Representatives; and MCC's Current, Past and Designate-President). As demand for membership always outstrips supply each year, there continues to be a substantial waiting list for Full Ordinary Membership, currently around 27 years.[[24]](#cite_note-HTJ-24) There are, however, ways to hasten the time it takes to become a full member: one may qualify as a Playing Member, or Out-Match Member (although this carries none of the privileges of membership, apart from being able to play for the club).[[24]](#cite_note-HTJ-24)

In addition, membership rules allow a certain number of people each year to be elected ahead of their turn; beneficiaries have included [Sir Mick Jagger](/wiki/Sir_Mick_Jagger "Sir Mick Jagger") and in 2018 then-[Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister "Prime Minister"), [Theresa, *now* Lady May](/wiki/Theresa_May "Theresa May"). MCC also awards limited honorary membership to people who have distinguished themselves in cricket. The club recognises achievement in [women's cricket](/wiki/Women%27s_cricket "Women's cricket") with, for example, [Charlotte Edwards](/wiki/Charlotte_Edwards "Charlotte Edwards") an inductee in 2012.[[24]](#cite_note-HTJ-24)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Controversies")]

In 2005 the MCC was criticised (including by a few of its own members)[[29]](#cite_note-29) for siding with the [England and Wales Cricket Board](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") (ECB) over the latter's decision to award television rights for [Test cricket](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket") to [British Sky Broadcasting](/wiki/British_Sky_Broadcasting "British Sky Broadcasting"), thus removing Test cricket from terrestrial television. The then-Secretary and Chief Executive of MCC, [Roger Knight](/wiki/Roger_Knight "Roger Knight"), represented the club on the board of the ECB and was party to this decision, prior to which Test cricket had been shown free to viewers on British television for over half a century.

MCC has the option of allowing members and other spectators to continue to bring a limited amount of alcoholic drinks into the ground at all matches. The [ICC](/wiki/International_Cricket_Council "International Cricket Council"), was attempting to implement a ban on this practice at all international matches around the world. MCC opted to write to the ICC on an annual basis to seek permission for members and spectators to import alcohol into [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The Secretary & Chief Executive of the Club has a seat on the administrative board of the [ECB](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") and it is believed that [Keith Bradshaw](/wiki/Keith_Bradshaw_(cricketer) "Keith Bradshaw (cricketer)") (Secretary & Chief Executive 2006–11) may have influenced the removal from office of [England Coach](/wiki/List_of_England_cricket_team_coaches "List of England cricket team coaches") [Duncan Fletcher](/wiki/Duncan_Fletcher "Duncan Fletcher") in April 2007.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

In 2012, MCC made headlines over a redevelopment plan, *Vision for Lord's*, that would have increased capacity but included construction of residential flats on some of the MCC site. Internal strife over the process of making a decision on the proposal led to the resignation of former Prime Minister [Sir John Major](/wiki/Sir_John_Major "Sir John Major") from MCC's Main Committee.[[32]](#cite_note-Tel-32)

In 2022, Guy Lavender, Secretary & Chief Executive of MCC, announced that the annual one-day [Oxford v Cambridge](/wiki/University_Match "University Match") and [Eton v Harrow](/wiki/Eton_v_Harrow "Eton v Harrow") matches, both of which have been played at Lord's since the early 19th century, would no longer be held at the ground, so as to make room in the fixture list for the finals of competitions for all universities and schools in pursuit of greater diversity. Following opposition from a majority of its membership, the club decided that the matches would continue to be held at Lord's until 2023 to allow time for further consultation.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In March 2023 it was announced that the fixtures would continue to be played at Lord's until at least 2027, following which there would be a review and a possible vote in 2028 on whether these historic matches should remain at Lord's.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

During the Second Test of the [2023 Ashes series](/wiki/2023_Ashes_series "2023 Ashes series") at Lord's, the MCC apologised to [Cricket Australia](/wiki/Cricket_Australia "Cricket Australia") and suspended three members for confronting [Australian players](/wiki/Australia_national_cricket_team "Australia national cricket team") walking through the Long Room.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Video emerged of MCC members shouting abuse at Australian players after [Jonny Bairstow](/wiki/Jonny_Bairstow "Jonny Bairstow") was [stumped](/wiki/Stumped "Stumped") in controversial circumstances, despite his dismissal being fair and legal.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Matches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Matches")]

MCC men's and women's teams play domestic matches throughout the spring and summer against teams from universities, schools, the Armed Forces and invitational teams such as the [Duchess of Rutland's XI](/wiki/Emma_Manners,_Duchess_of_Rutland "Emma Manners, Duchess of Rutland").[[37]](#cite_note-37) The men's team tour internationally four times per year, and the women's team tour every other year.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/MCC_Coaching_book.jpg/250px-MCC_Coaching_book.jpg)](/wiki/File:MCC_Coaching_book.jpg)

[MCC coaching manuals](/wiki/MCC_coaching_manual "MCC coaching manual")

## Coaching

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Coaching")]

MCC has long had a deep involvement in coaching cricket. The club's head coach leads an extensive operation involving the running of an indoor-cricket school and a team of coaches in England and around the world. The club has traditionally produced a coaching manual, the *[MCC Cricket Coaching Book](/wiki/MCC_Cricket_Coaching_Book "MCC Cricket Coaching Book")*, a bible for cricket skills, and runs training programmes for young cricketers, including many at its Lord's Indoor Centre.[[38]](#cite_note-38) MCC continues to tour around England, playing matches against various state and private schools. This tradition has been followed since the 19th century. The club has other sporting interests with both a [real tennis](/wiki/Real_tennis "Real tennis") and a [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)") court on site at Lord's, and [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess"), [bridge](/wiki/Bridge_(card_game) "Bridge (card game)") and [backgammon](/wiki/Backgammon "Backgammon") societies.

## Club colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Club colours")]

Since [Edwardian times](/wiki/Edwardian_era "Edwardian era") at the beginning of the 20th century, the Marylebone Cricket Club organised [England](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") [Test matches](/wiki/Test_cricket "Test cricket"), touring overseas officially as *MCC* up till the [1976/77 tour of India](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_India_and_Sri_Lanka_in_1976%E2%80%9377 "English cricket team in India and Sri Lanka in 1976–77").[[39]](#cite_note-39) [England cricketers](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team") last wore MCC's distinctive red and yellow-striped colours during the [tour of New Zealand in 1996/97](/wiki/English_cricket_team_in_New_Zealand_in_1996%E2%80%9397 "English cricket team in New Zealand in 1996–97").

The true provenance of the [club colours](/wiki/Sporting_colours "Sporting colours") is (and probably will remain) unsubstantiated, but the MCC originally sported [sky blue](/wiki/Sky_blue "Sky blue") until well into the 19th century.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Shield_of_arms_of_The_Reverend_Lord_Frederick_de_Vere_Beauclerk.png/250px-Shield_of_arms_of_The_Reverend_Lord_Frederick_de_Vere_Beauclerk.png)](/wiki/File:Shield_of_arms_of_The_Reverend_Lord_Frederick_de_Vere_Beauclerk.png)

[Arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of the Revd Lord Frederick Beauclerk

The Marylebone Cricket Club eventually settled on its now well-recognised colours of scarlet and gold,[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42) otherwise described as "bacon and egg".[[43]](#cite_note-43) One purported theory is that they derive from the [J&W Nicholson & Co](/wiki/J%26W_Nicholson_%26_Co "J&W Nicholson & Co"). [gin](/wiki/Gin "Gin")-[distilling](/wiki/Distilling "Distilling") [family](/wiki/Nicholson_baronets "Nicholson baronets"), whose Chairman and MCC Committee member, [William Nicholson](/wiki/William_Nicholson_(distiller) "William Nicholson (distiller)") [MP](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (1825–1909) financed the purchase of Lord's freehold in 1866.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

More likely is that MCC's [red](/wiki/Gules "Gules") and [gold](/wiki/Or_(heraldry) "Or (heraldry)") [colours](/wiki/Sporting_colours "Sporting colours") have evolved from the [heraldic](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") [livery](/wiki/Livery "Livery") ([racing) colours](/wiki/Horse_racing_colours_in_Great_Britain "Horse racing colours in Great Britain") of key noble patrons, such as [the Dukes of Richmond](/wiki/Charles_Lennox,_4th_Duke_of_Richmond "Charles Lennox, 4th Duke of Richmond") and kinsman, [Lord Frederick Beauclerk](/wiki/Lord_Frederick_Beauclerk "Lord Frederick Beauclerk"), President of Marylebone Cricket Club in 1826.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

## Image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Image")]

Previously perceived as too [traditional](/wiki/Tradition "Tradition") and [elitest](/wiki/Elite "Elite"), MCC has adapted with the times making concerted efforts to modernise.[[46]](#cite_note-46) "It would be overstating things to claim that the MCC has come full circle," admitted Andrew Miller at the beginning of October 2008, "but at a time of massive upheaval in the world game, .. [its] colours cease to represent everything that is wrong with cricket, and instead have become a touchstone for those whose greatest fear is the erosion of the game's traditional values".[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Legal status

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Legal status")]

Prior to 2013, MCC was an [unincorporated](/wiki/Unincorporated_associations_in_English_law "Unincorporated associations in English law") [private members' club](/wiki/Gentlemen%27s_club "Gentlemen's club"), which status had several limitations. Since an unincorporated association cannot be a legal entity, MCC could not own property (such as Lord's Cricket Ground) [in its own name](/wiki/Suo_jure "Suo jure"). MCC could not sue anybody, nor indeed be sued (any legal action having to be taken against the Secretary & Chief Executive personally). Any successful financial claim made against the Club would have to be met by the Committee with liability extending to all MCC members for any shortfall in funds. The Club therefore convened a [Special General Meeting](/wiki/Special_General_Meeting "Special General Meeting") in June 2012 to propose petitioning the [Queen-in-Council](/wiki/Queen-in-Council "Queen-in-Council") for the incorporation of Marylebone Cricket Club by [Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter").[[48]](#cite_note-48)
Resulting from this petition, in December 2012 the MCC was granted a Royal Charter, two previous attempts having been unsuccessful.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Removing many of the barriers to simplify its administration, MCC became incorporated enabling it to hold assets in its own right, including [Lord's Cricket Ground](/wiki/Lord%27s_Cricket_Ground "Lord's Cricket Ground"), instead of via [custodian trusteeship](/wiki/Trustee "Trustee"). The Royal Charter also removed financial liability for any shortfall from its individual members, as owners of Marylebone Cricket Club.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## MCC Universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: MCC Universities")]

From 2005 the MCC funded six university cricket academies known as the MCC Universities (MCCUs), which had previously been funded (from 2000) by the [England and Wales Cricket Board](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") (ECB). (Prior to 2010 they were known as the University Centres of Cricketing Excellence, or UCCEs.) These were based at [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge_University_Cricket_Club "Cambridge University Cricket Club"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff_MCC_University "Cardiff MCC University"), [Durham](/wiki/Durham_MCC_University "Durham MCC University"), [Leeds/Bradford](/wiki/Leeds/Bradford_MCC_University "Leeds/Bradford MCC University"), [Loughborough](/wiki/Loughborough_MCC_University "Loughborough MCC University") and [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford_University_Cricket_Club "Oxford University Cricket Club"), and incorporated a total of thirteen universities.[[51]](#cite_note-51) From 2012 all six MCCUs held first-class status. Each MCCU played a trio of matches against professional county teams at the beginning of each season, with first-class status conferred on the first two of these matches.[[52]](#cite_note-52) In 2018, the MCC and ECB announced that the ECB would be resuming responsibility for funding the university centres from 2020 and would run a tender process for new cities to join the scheme. The change was also said to be likely to result in more [T20](/wiki/Twenty20 "Twenty20") cricket in the programme.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54) Despite the ECB having resumed funding, the university teams continue to use the MCC University names.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Officers of the MCC

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Officers of the MCC")]

MCC Presidents are appointed to serve an annual term (biennial terms in the 1990s), exceptions being extensions of office during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

Appointed President of Marylebone Cricket Club in 1914, [Lord Hawke](/wiki/Martin_Hawke,_7th_Baron_Hawke "Martin Hawke, 7th Baron Hawke") was asked to remain in post till the end of [the Great War](/wiki/The_Great_War "The Great War"),[[56]](#cite_note-P&D-56) when [Lord's](/wiki/Lord%27s "Lord's") was used for military (including training and recreational) purposes. Problems frequently arose but, in *Wisden's* view, Hawke provided "the greatest help in giving wise counsel towards their solution",[[57]](#cite_note-wistrib-57) liaising with [Henry (*then* Lord) Forster](/wiki/Henry_Forster,_1st_Baron_Forster "Henry Forster, 1st Baron Forster"), whom he nominated as his successor for 1919.[[58]](#cite_note-58)
Lord Hawke's tenure was exceeded only by that of [Stanley Christopherson](/wiki/Stanley_Christopherson "Stanley Christopherson"), appointed in 1939 and remaining *in situ* for seven years, before being succeeded in 1945 by [General Sir Ronald Adam](/wiki/Ronald_Forbes_Adam "Ronald Forbes Adam").[[59]](#cite_note-BWC49-59)

In his *Barclay's World of Cricket* essay, [E. W. Swanton](/wiki/E._W._Swanton "E. W. Swanton") declares that "there is no pretence of democracy about" MCC's Presidents, commenting on how few were [untitled](/wiki/Nobility "Nobility") up till the Second World War and that belonging to the aristocracy was more important than any cricketing prowess.[[60]](#cite_note-60) His observation reflected societal change, although [Prince Philip](/wiki/Prince_Philip "Prince Philip"), former PM [Sir Alec Douglas-Home](/wiki/Sir_Alec_Douglas-Home "Sir Alec Douglas-Home"), [the Duke of Norfolk](/wiki/Bernard_Fitzalan-Howard,_16th_Duke_of_Norfolk "Bernard Fitzalan-Howard, 16th Duke of Norfolk") and his son-in-law [Lord Cowdrey](/wiki/Lord_Cowdrey "Lord Cowdrey"), served as Presidents of MCC in the latter half of the 20th century.

21st-century MCC Presidents so far include many former professional cricket players such as [Tom Graveney](/wiki/Tom_Graveney "Tom Graveney"), [Derek Underwood](/wiki/Derek_Underwood "Derek Underwood"), [Mike Gatting](/wiki/Mike_Gatting "Mike Gatting"), [Matthew Fleming](/wiki/Matthew_Fleming "Matthew Fleming") and [Kumar Sangakkara](/wiki/Kumar_Sangakkara "Kumar Sangakkara") (nominated by predecessor, Anthony Wreford), becoming the first Asian President of Marylebone Cricket Club in 2019.[[61]](#cite_note-kumar-president-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

[Presidents](/wiki/President_(corporate_title) "President (corporate title)") of MCC are required to nominate a successor at the Annual General Meeting (AGM) during their term of office.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The MCC Chairman and Treasurer each serve a three-year term, both being appointed by the MCC Committee (subject to approval of the voting members), and can serve successive terms.   
The [Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_(title) "Secretary (title)") & [Chief Executive](/wiki/Chief_Executive "Chief Executive") to Marylebone Cricket Club (a combined role) is the Club's senior employee, appointed solely by the MCC Committee.

### MCC Secretaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marylebone_Cricket_Club&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: MCC Secretaries")]

The first [Secretary](/wiki/Secretary_(title) "Secretary (title)") to the Marylebone Cricket Club was engaged in 1822,[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66) a title which changed in 2000, under [Roger Knight](/wiki/Roger_Knight "Roger Knight")'s tenure, to become Secretary and Chief Executive. Holders of this office have been:

* [Benjamin Aislabie](/wiki/Benjamin_Aislabie "Benjamin Aislabie") (1822–1842)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Roger Kynaston](/wiki/Roger_Kynaston "Roger Kynaston") (1842–1858)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Alfred Baillie](/wiki/Alfred_Baillie "Alfred Baillie") (1858–1863)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Fitz Fitzgerald](/wiki/Robert_Allan_Fitzgerald "Robert Allan Fitzgerald") (1863–1876)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Henry Perkins](/wiki/Henry_Perkins_(cricketer) "Henry Perkins (cricketer)") (1876–1898)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Sir Francis Lacey](/wiki/Francis_Lacey "Francis Lacey") (1898–1926)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [William Findlay](/wiki/William_Findlay_(cricketer,_born_1880) "William Findlay (cricketer, born 1880)") (1926–1936)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Rowan Rait Kerr](/wiki/Rowan_Rait_Kerr "Rowan Rait Kerr") (1936–1952)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Ronnie Aird](/wiki/Ronnie_Aird "Ronnie Aird") (1952–1962)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Billy Griffith](/wiki/Billy_Griffith "Billy Griffith") (1962–1974)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [Jack Bailey](/wiki/J._A._Bailey "J. A. Bailey") (1974–1987)[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESwanton198654-66)
* [John Stephenson](/wiki/John_Robin_Stephenson "John Robin Stephenson") (1987–1994)[[67]](#cite_note-67)
* [Roger Knight](/wiki/Roger_Knight "Roger Knight") (1994–2006)[[68]](#cite_note-68)
* [Keith Bradshaw](/wiki/Keith_Bradshaw_(cricketer) "Keith Bradshaw (cricketer)") (2006–2011)[[69]](#cite_note-69)
* [Derek Brewer](/wiki/Derek_Brewer_(cricket_administrator) "Derek Brewer (cricket administrator)") (2011–2017)[[70]](#cite_note-70)
* Guy Lavender (2017–2024)[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)
* Robert Lawson (2025–)[[73]](#cite_note-73)
